skill.name: framework-migration-code-migrate
skill.description: Guides codebase migrations between frameworks, languages, versions, APIs, databases, and deployment strategies.
skill.summary: Plan, automate, test, and roll back complex software migrations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical combinations, command execution, network access, filesystem access, credential access, and weak cryptography. Review found no malicious intent, no prompt injection, and no credential exfiltration; most severe hits are Markdown code fences, generated code samples, or ordinary migration pattern text. The skill should publish with a warning because copied examples can read local source trees, write output files, call REST endpoints, and load an external CDN script.

medium_findings.title: Generated Examples Can Make Outbound API Requests
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for network behavior in sample code. The REST-to-GraphQL migration example creates resolvers that call mapped REST endpoints with fetch, which is legitimate for API migration but requires endpoint validation when copied into a project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch calls are direct and semantically part of generated resolver examples. I did not find evidence that the skill itself sends data to an unauthorized endpoint.

medium_findings.title: Generated CLI Example Writes User-Named Files
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for filesystem behavior in sample code. The migration CLI example writes analysis output to a path supplied by the user, so copied code could overwrite files without additional safeguards.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write call is explicit and the output path is configurable. The context is a migration utility, so this is an operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Examples Recursively Read Source Code Trees
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for broad local file access in sample code. The analyzer walks a source directory and reads matching files, which is expected for migration analysis but should be limited to approved repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file reads are direct and intentional in migration examples. No evidence found that the skill reads files automatically outside user-requested migration work.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The reported Ruby or shell backtick locations are Markdown code fences, template literals, or resource references, not executable shell commands in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are Markdown delimiters, JavaScript template output, or references to the playbook file. No subprocess, shell, eval, or command invocation was found in those locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The reported weak cryptography lines contain migration risk labels, framework terms, or ordinary text; no cryptographic API or weak algorithm use is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hash, cipher, certificate, or key handling code at the cited locations. The static matches are caused by unrelated words and severity labels.

low_findings.title: Credential And Critical Combination Heuristics Are Unconfirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious intent. No evidence found of credential collection, secret file access, encoded payloads, exfiltration, or prompt injection in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited credential and combination indicators resolve to iterator migration text, REST parameter construction, a migration identifier, and a CDN URL. The examples still justify a medium warning due to filesystem and network behavior.

low_findings.title: Generated Dashboard Loads External CDN Script
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for an external URL in generated sample HTML. The dashboard example loads Chart.js from jsDelivr, which is common but introduces third-party dependency risk when copied.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded external script URL is explicit. The context is generated dashboard sample code, not hidden runtime behavior in the skill itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Fetch-Based Resolver Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook includes fetch-based resolver examples that can send request data to configured REST endpoints if used without validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in the generated example. It is not evidence of hidden network activity by the skill package.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Local File Write Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The generated CLI example writes analysis results to a user-selected output path and should include overwrite checks in real use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem write is explicit and user controlled. It is a normal migration-tool operation with moderate misuse risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CDN Dependency In Generated HTML
dangerous_patterns.description: The dashboard sample uses a third-party CDN script, which should be pinned, vendored, or reviewed before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL is hardcoded in sample HTML. The finding is limited to generated code, not an active skill behavior.

content.value_statement: Framework and platform migrations often fail from unclear scope, missing tests, and weak rollback plans. This skill creates structured migration plans, examples, validation steps, and rollback guidance.
actual_capabilities: Assesses source code size, dependencies, patterns, and migration complexity.
Creates phased migration plans with milestones, risks, and validation criteria.
Provides example transformations for React to Vue and Python 2 to Python 3 migrations.
Outlines REST to GraphQL and SQL to NoSQL migration approaches.
Defines comparison testing, performance checks, rollback triggers, and progress monitoring.
limitations: Examples are templates and need adaptation for each repository.
It does not guarantee semantic equivalence after automated transformations.
Generated scripts need review before they read, write, or call external services.
It cannot replace framework-specific migration documentation for edge cases.

use_cases.title: Plan a Framework Rewrite
use_cases.description: Create phases, risks, test gates, and rollback steps before moving a frontend application to a new framework.

use_cases.title: Upgrade Legacy Language Code
use_cases.description: Identify syntax changes, deprecated APIs, and test coverage needed for a major language version upgrade.

use_cases.title: Design API or Database Transitions
use_cases.description: Map REST endpoints, GraphQL schema changes, SQL tables, NoSQL documents, and verification checks.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Migration Scope
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess my project migration from [source stack] to [target stack]. Ask for missing details, then summarize scope, risks, and first steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Migration Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a phased migration plan for [application type]. Include milestones, owners, testing gates, rollback criteria, and release sequencing.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Transformation Guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these representative files and propose safe transformations from [old pattern] to [new pattern]. Include manual review points and validation tests.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Migration Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this migration plan for production readiness. Check data integrity, performance, observability, rollback, security, and stakeholder communication.

output_examples.input: Migrate a React application to Vue with minimal downtime.
output_examples.output: A phased plan covering component inventory, compatibility mapping, pilot migration, shared utilities, parallel testing, release gates, and rollback triggers.

output_examples.input: Upgrade a Python 2 service to Python 3.
output_examples.output: A risk assessment with syntax changes, dependency checks, automated conversion guidance, compatibility tests, deployment sequencing, and rollback steps.

output_examples.input: Move selected REST endpoints to GraphQL.
output_examples.output: An API migration outline with endpoint mapping, schema design, resolver validation, client migration phases, performance checks, and monitoring needs.

best_practices: Run generated scripts in dry-run mode on a disposable branch first.
Require tests that compare old and new behavior before cutover.
Define rollback triggers before production migration starts.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated migration scripts without code review.
Do not migrate data without backups and integrity checks.
Do not remove legacy paths before production behavior is verified.

faq.question: Can this skill perform a migration automatically?
faq.answer: It can draft plans and example scripts, but engineers must review and run changes safely.

faq.question: Does it support framework upgrades?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for framework changes, language upgrades, API migration, and database migration.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What inputs improve the output?
faq.answer: Provide source and target stacks, repository structure, constraints, release deadlines, test coverage, and rollback requirements.

faq.question: Is it safe to run generated scripts directly?
faq.answer: No. Review scripts first, use a disposable branch, and confirm file, network, and data access behavior.

faq.question: Does it replace official migration guides?
faq.answer: No. Use it to structure work, then verify details against official documentation and project-specific tests.
